name: Camp Lawton Staff App Ticker Feeds
purpose: Top-of-site ticker for the Camp Lawton Staff app. It gives the staff portal life and flavor outside of camp while keeping items broadly staff-safe, outdoors-adjacent, useful, wholesome, and clickable.

primaryAudience: parents or prospects if the app is visible to them
designAssumptions.tickerBehavior: Display only a short title or sentence. Clicking opens the RSS item URL, source URL, or internal app route.
designAssumptions.seriousUpdates: Operational notices may surface in a separate alert lane; life/safety updates should not rely only on ticker rotation.
designAssumptions.defaultTone: Useful, calm, wholesome, outdoorsy, lightly fun, never doom-scroll bait.

displayRules.rssDisplayText: Use the RSS item title by default. Do not display summaries in the top ticker.
displayRules.aiDisplayText: Use one generated short sentence or title only.
displayRules.clickBehavior: Every active item must include a URL. External links open source item; offline content opens internal app route.

rotationRules.dadJokeCap: No more than 1 dad joke per 12 displayed ticker items.
rotationRules.operationalItems: Operational candidates may interrupt the flavor ticker only when current and relevant; otherwise route to normal flavor rotation or suppress.

flavor.description: Normal rotating ticker: camp flavor, nature wonder, Scouting, local outdoors, wholesome fun.

operational_candidate.description: Potentially practical updates such as road status, fire restrictions, council updates, flag status, closures, or weather/access notices. Promote to alert banner only if current and relevant.

rssFeeds.title: On Scouting
rssFeeds.notes: Scout-friendly program ideas, updates, family adventure, cheerful leadership.

rssFeeds.title: Grand Canyon National Park News Releases
rssFeeds.notes: Arizona/NPS updates; use closures and hazards as calm safety notices.

rssFeeds.title: Grand Canyon Backcountry Updates and Closures
rssFeeds.notes: Practical backcountry conditions, closures, and safety context.
rssFeeds.operationalNote: Backcountry closure-style updates; mainly useful as outdoor safety context, not Camp Lawton operations.

rssFeeds.title: Grand Canyon Park Newspapers
rssFeeds.notes: Slower evergreen park updates.

rssFeeds.title: NASA Earth Observatory Image of the Day
rssFeeds.notes: Awe-and-wonder Earth imagery with one plain-English fact.

rssFeeds.title: NASA Earth Observatory Natural Events
rssFeeds.notes: Natural events from space; keep calm, scientific, and not alarmist.

rssFeeds.title: Good News Network — Animals
rssFeeds.notes: Animal rescues, wildlife wins, and 'aww, humanity is okay' moments.

rssFeeds.title: Good News Network — Inspiring
rssFeeds.notes: Short human kindness and perseverance stories.

rssFeeds.title: Good News Network — Science
rssFeeds.notes: Science wins that feel hopeful, curious, or useful.

rssFeeds.title: Smithsonian Magazine — Science & Nature
rssFeeds.notes: Nature, animals, fossils, space, and discovery; avoid heavy medical stories.

rssFeeds.title: Smithsonian Magazine — Travel
rssFeeds.notes: Interesting places, history, culture, and gentle wanderlust.

rssFeeds.title: Outside Online — Adventure
rssFeeds.notes: Outdoor adventure headlines; filter for inspiring, practical, non-gory items.

rssFeeds.title: Outside Online — Travel
rssFeeds.notes: Trip ideas and outdoor travel; filter hard for wholesome and useful.

rssFeeds.title: REI Co-op Journal
rssFeeds.notes: Outdoor how-to, stewardship, gear basics, beginner-friendly adventure.

rssFeeds.title: Scout Life
rssFeeds.notes: Kid-friendly Scouting, outdoor skills, projects, hobbies, and fun facts.

rssFeeds.title: Scouting Wire
rssFeeds.notes: Official-ish Scouting news and national updates; prefer cheerful program and service items.

rssFeeds.title: Scouting Newsroom
rssFeeds.notes: Official Scouting America press/news items; use only constructive program, service, and youth achievement items.

rssFeeds.title: NASA Image of the Day
rssFeeds.notes: Space awe, beautiful images, exploration, sky-watching, and gentle science curiosity.

rssFeeds.title: NASA News
rssFeeds.notes: NASA missions and science news; filter for wonder, exploration, and educational value.

rssFeeds.title: Atlas Obscura Latest
rssFeeds.notes: Odd places, hidden history, travel curiosity, and delightful weirdness.

rssFeeds.title: Good News Network
rssFeeds.notes: Hopeful human, animal, science, and community stories.

rssFeeds.title: Arizona Daily Star — Outdoors
rssFeeds.notes: Southern Arizona outdoors, trails, wildlife, parks, and local recreation.
rssFeeds.operationalNote: Local outdoor updates around Tucson and nearby public lands; filter aggressively.

rssFeeds.title: Leave No Trace
rssFeeds.notes: Stewardship, low-impact camping, trail kindness, and outdoor ethics.

rssFeeds.title: USFS Region 3 News and Events
rssFeeds.notes: Arizona/New Mexico national forest updates; include useful access, safety, stewardship, and volunteer items.
rssFeeds.operationalNote: Regional forest notices that may affect travel, restrictions, closures, or fire conditions.

rssFeeds.title: Arizona Game & Fish
rssFeeds.notes: Arizona wildlife, conservation, fishing, boating, and outdoor education updates.
rssFeeds.operationalNote: Arizona wildlife and outdoor notices; useful for wildlife safety/context.

rssFeeds.title: Outside Online
rssFeeds.notes: Outdoor lifestyle and adventure; filter aggressively for useful, wholesome, non-clickbait items.

rssFeeds.title: Audubon
rssFeeds.notes: Birds, habitats, conservation wins, and birdwatching curiosity.

rssFeeds.title: All About Birds
rssFeeds.notes: Bird facts, birdwatching, citizen science, and gentle nature learning.

rssFeeds.title: Astronomy Picture of the Day
rssFeeds.notes: Daily sky wonder, astronomy facts, and cosmic perspective.

rssFeeds.title: EarthSky
rssFeeds.notes: Skywatching, astronomy, Earth science, and night-sky events.

rssFeeds.title: National Archives News
rssFeeds.notes: History, civic memory, exhibits, documents, and educational anniversaries.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Tiny Awe
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: From recent nature, space, Earth, animal, fossil, or science sources, choose one broad-audience-safe item. Return one wonder-filled ticker line under 110 characters. Avoid disaster, politics, or alarmist framing.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Scout Spark
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Find one Scout-appropriate leadership, service, skill, or outdoor idea. Return one friendly action prompt under 110 characters and link to the source item.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Trail Kindness
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Generate one tiny good-turn prompt for camp staff under 95 characters. It must be kind, practical, non-preachy, and safe for teen staff. Link internally to camp culture.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Arizona Outdoors Watch
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Summarize one current Arizona outdoor item relevant to trails, forests, wildlife, weather, access, closures, or stewardship. Under 110 characters. Avoid tragedy unless it is a practical closure/safety notice.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Learn One Thing
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Create one staff-useful micro-learning prompt under 100 characters, preferably about knots, maps, Leave No Trace, hydration, sun safety, teaching, or leadership. Link internally.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Good News Nature
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Choose one positive nature, wildlife, conservation, science, or community-service story. Return one calm upbeat ticker line under 110 characters. Avoid celebrity or partisan stories.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Arizona Half-Staff Flag Status
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Check current Arizona half-staff flag status. Only display a ticker item when flags should be lowered today or tomorrow. Use a plain, respectful sentence under 100 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Scouting America Watch
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Find a current Scouting America item that is useful, encouraging, or program-relevant for camp staff. Avoid controversy and legal/political topics. Under 110 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: World Scouting Newsroom
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Find one broad, positive world Scouting item related to service, outdoor adventure, leadership, or youth development. Under 110 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Catalina Council Watch
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Search for Catalina Council updates relevant to Camp Lawton staff, council events, training, registration, service, or outdoor programs. Under 110 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Mt. Lemmon Watch
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Search for Mt. Lemmon road, weather, fire restriction, closure, nature, or community updates. Use operational lane only for closures/restrictions. Under 110 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Summerhaven Watch
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Search for Summerhaven updates relevant to travel, weather, access, community events, wildfire recovery, or mountain context. Under 110 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Word of the Day
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Fetch the Dictionary.com word of the day. Display as: “Word of the day: WORD — very short definition.” Keep under 100 characters.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: Riddle of the Day
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Fetch one riddle and its answer. Display only the riddle as a short ticker line; the click target should reveal the answer or go to the riddle source.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: This Day in Scouting History
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Find one Scouting-history item for today if a solid source exists. If none is found, do not display. Keep under 110 characters and link to the source.

aiAggregationFeeds.title: National Day Calendar
aiAggregationFeeds.prompt: Fetch today’s National Day Calendar items. Display only broadly wholesome, camp-friendly, food/fun/service/nature items. Skip commercial, political, adult, or weirdly specific items.

offlineTicker.title: Pocket challenge: tie a square knot slowly enough that you could teach it to someone else.
Try the two-minute campsite scan: sky, ground, water, gear, buddy.
A good patrol leader asks: Who needs help before I start my own task?
Campfire rule: leave people warmer than you found them.
Leave No Trace tiny win: pick up one thing that is not yours, even if nobody sees it.
The best campsite looks boring after you leave. That is the magic trick.
Respect wildlife means giving animals enough space to keep being animals.
A quiet trail is a gift to the next hiker, the birds, and your own brain.
Water check: if your bottle is still full after an hour outside, your plan is bad.
Arizona summer wisdom: shade is not cowardice. Shade is strategy.
Thunder rule: when thunder roars, go indoors. Dramatic skies are not worth becoming a cautionary tale.
If your socks are miserable, your hike is secretly over. Fix small problems early.
Saguaro arms usually appear only after decades. Desert patience is undefeated.
Ravens can solve puzzles, recognize faces, and absolutely judge your campsite snack habits.
Pinecones open and close with humidity. Tiny wooden weather stations, basically.
Lichen is a partnership between fungus and algae or bacteria. Nature loves a weird roommate situation.
Look up tonight: even one visible star is a reminder that the universe bothered to sparkle.
The Moon is not changing shape; we are just seeing different sunlight on the same loyal rock.
Cloud check: if they are stacking tall and dark, the sky may be moving from scenery to plot.
Take one slow breath outside. Congratulations, you have briefly become a plant with opinions.
Name five things you can hear. The world is usually less empty than your brain claims.
A short walk still counts. The ground does not require a minimum purchase.
Good turn idea: refill someone’s water, carry one extra thing, or notice who got quiet.
Wholesome mission: compliment effort, not talent. People can use that kind of fuel.
Be the person who says, “I saved you a seat.” It is tiny. It matters.
Camp math: one marshmallow is a snack, two is dessert, three is a personal journey.
A compass does not care how confident you sound. Honestly, admirable boundaries.
Trail mix is just cereal that got accepted into a rugged college.
If the camp chair folds you instead of the other way around, tradition requires laughter.
Someone once looked at a mountain and thought, “Yes, but what if we added paperwork?” Scouting was inevitable.
Old trails are stories written by feet. Walk gently; you are editing the next sentence.
Peanut butter is basically camp duct tape: adhesive, useful, and somehow everywhere.
Hot cocoa tastes better outside because your soul knows it earned a mug.
Mini quest: identify one bird call today. Wrong guesses allowed; curiosity gets full credit.
Mini quest: find a leaf, rock, or cloud that looks like it is trying to be something else.
Mini quest: teach one tiny outdoor skill in under sixty seconds.
Mini quest: ask someone for their favorite camp memory and actually let them tell it.
Hydrate before you feel thirsty. Arizona does not grade on a curve.
Teach the skill, then step back. Scouts remember the part they got to do.
Notice the quiet staffer. A small check-in can save a whole day.
Fix small gear problems early. Blisters, sunburns, and bad moods love procrastination.
A clean program area is a silent lesson in leadership.
Leave No Trace starts before the hike: plan, pack, and prevent the mess.
Tonight’s sky is part of the program, even when nobody put it on the schedule.
The best staff questions are simple: Who needs help? What happens next? What can wait?
Camp Lawton has a long memory. Add something worth remembering.
Campfire magic is mostly timing, kindness, and somebody remembering the matches.
Did you hear about the circus fire? It was in tents!
How do you catch a squirrel? Climb a tree and act like a nut!
Did you hear about the guy who invented Lifesavers? They say he made a mint!
Why don't eggs tell jokes? They might crack up!
What did the big flower say to the little flower? "Hi, bud!"
I went to buy some camouflage pants, but I couldn't find any.
What did the grape say when it got stepped on? Nothing, it just let out a little wine.
I used to have a job at a calendar factory, but I got fired because I took a couple of days off.
What do you call a snowman with a six-pack? An abdominal snowman!
Why don't skeletons fight each other? They don't have the guts!
Did you hear about the restaurant on the moon? Great food, no atmosphere!
What did one wall say to the other wall? I'll meet you at the corner!
Why did the math book look sad? Because it had too many problems!
What did one hat say to the other hat? You stay here, I'll go on ahead!
Why did the coffee file a police report? It got mugged!
I was going to tell you a joke about time travel, but you didn't like it.
I used to be a baker, but I couldn't make enough dough.
Did you hear about the guy who got hit in the head with a can of soda? He was lucky it was a soft drink.
I'm writing a book about glue, but I'm stuck on the first chapter.
What did one plate whisper to the other plate? Dinner is on me.
Why did the golfer bring two pairs of pants? In case he got a hole in one.
Two sheep walk into a—baaaa.
Try the seafood diet—you see food, then you eat it.
Did you hear the rumor about butter? Well, I'm not going to go spreading it!
What's Forrest Gump's password? 1forrest1
What state is known for its small drinks? Minnesota.
What does a nosey pepper do? It gets jalapeño business.
If two vegetarians get in an argument, is it still called beef?
I love telling Dad jokes. Sometimes, he even laughs.
What's 90 degrees but covered with ice? The North and South Poles.
What happened when the blue ship and the red ship collided at sea? Their crews were marooned.
What's the difference between the bird flu and the swine flu? One requires tweetment and the other an oinkmen…
What do you call a line of men waiting to get haircuts? A barberqueue.
Why do seagulls fly over the sea? If they flew over the bay, they would be bagels.
I'm thinking I should do lunges to stay in shape. That would be a big step forward.
What did the baby corn say to the mama corn? Where's popcorn?
What vegetable is cool, but not that cool? Radish.
What do you call two monkeys who share an Amazon Prime account? Prime mates.
What do you call a beehive without an exit? Un-bee-lievable.
Why did the football coach go to the bank? To get his quarter back.
Why can't a leopard hide? He's always spotted.
Air used to be free at the gas station, now it costs 2.50. You want to know why? Inflation.
I tried to get a smart car the other day but they sold out too fast. Why? I guess I'm just a bit slow.
Did you hear about the claustrophobic astronaut? He just wanted a bit more space.
Why did the orange lose the race? It ran out of juice.
How you fix a broken pumpkin? With a pumpkin patch.
Why are fish so smart? They live in schools!
What's the best thing about Switzerland? I don't know, but the flag is a big plus.

implementationNotes: For RSS entries, store item.title and item.link. The ticker should show item.title only and click through to item.link.
For AI aggregation entries, the generator must return both shortTitleOrSentence and a verified click URL.
For offline entries, click through to internal routes such as /wiki/training/outdoor-skills or /fun/dad-jokes.
Do not use the disabledOfflineArchive in production rotation unless a human reviews and enables specific items.
If title filtering removes too much content, loosen includeKeywords by category rather than removing excludeKeywords globally.

changeLog.summary: Reshaped merged feed list for Camp Lawton Staff app ticker: added audience assumptions, weighted categories, required click URLs, operational candidate lane, safer filters, capped dad jokes, and disabled archive for broad-audience-unfit jokes.
Added user-supplied RSS feeds, AI query recipes, and offline dad jokes; deduped exact RSS URLs and exact offline text.
